name: Purcell-Griffin Mine
short_description: BLM rockhounding site in the Sierra Nevada foothills near Mariposa offering opportunities to find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in a historic gold mining region.
description:
The Purcell-Griffin Mine area sits within California's renowned Sierra Nevada foothills rockhounding district, where you can search for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens on public BLM land. This location offers moderate terrain at 1,687 feet elevation with accessible collecting opportunities typical of the Mother Lode region's mineralogical legacy.

The area preserves evidence of 19th-century prospecting activity, with scattered mine workings and exposures of the granitic rocks that define the Sierra Nevada batholith. The quartz veins and feldspathic host rock that attracted early miners continue to yield quality specimens for modern rockhounders.

Collecting here is straightforward for beginners and experienced rockhounders alike. The moderate slope terrain provides good access to mineralized exposures and old mine dumps where weathering has exposed fresh mineral surfaces. Most productive collecting occurs on BLM land surrounding the historical mine workings.

Visitors should plan for typical Sierra Nevada foothill conditions: warm days during collecting season, and respect for the historical mine structures. The location combines educational geology with genuine collecting potential, making it a worthwhile destination for those exploring California's mining heritage.
history: The Purcell-Griffin Mine is part of the larger Mariposa County mining district that saw intensive gold prospecting beginning in the 1850s. The granitic rocks exposed here formed during Mesozoic batholith emplacement, with subsequent hydrothermal activity concentrating quartz and feldspar minerals in veins and pegmatites that attracted prospectors seeking not only gold but also mineral specimens.
tips: Search the old mine dump areas and exposed hillside cuts where weathering has broken down host rock and exposed fresh quartz and feldspar crystals - these locations show mineralization most clearly. Bring a rock hammer and chisel to carefully extract specimens from quartz veins running through the granitic outcrops. Look for milky white to clear quartz points and pink to gray feldspar crystals in the exposed pegmatitic rock; specimens typically range from fist-sized to several inches across. Pack plenty of water as the exposed terrain offers little shade and afternoon heat can be significant.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,687 feet with moderate slope. Terrain consists of exposed granitic rock with sparse chaparral and scattered oak. Walking distance from parking to productive areas typically 0.25 to 1 mile across uneven rocky ground. Footing is rocky with loose material common, requiring sturdy boots and careful step placement.
best_season: October - April. Summer months bring extreme heat to the Sierra foothills, making mid-day collecting uncomfortable. Fall through spring offers moderate temperatures and easier conditions for hiking the rocky terrain. Spring wildflowers also enhance the experience.
written_directions: From Mariposa, take California State Route 49 northeast toward Coulterville for approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM access points and parking pullouts in the area near the historical mining sites. GPS coordinates 37.662247, -120.195612 mark the Purcell-Griffin Mine vicinity. Access is via BLM land with unsigned pullouts along county roads; consult BLM maps or contact the Mariposa BLM office for current access information.
road_conditions: Unpaved county roads with washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle recommended but passenger cars can access pullouts in dry conditions. Avoid after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Informal BLM pullouts along county roads. No developed parking area. Space for 2-4 vehicles at typical pullout sites.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No collecting within posted mine claim boundaries. Respect historical structures and do not enter abandoned mine shafts.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No collecting within posted claim boundaries
Do not enter abandoned mine shafts
nearest_services: Mariposa, approximately 10 miles west. Full services including gas, food, water, and supplies available in town.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with loose material throughout. Moderate slope requires good footing and sturdy hiking boots. No wheelchair access. Old mine workings present tripping hazards. Not suitable for those with limited mobility, but manageable for most hikers in reasonable fitness.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Purcell-Griffin Mine near Mariposa?
faq.answer: You can find quartz (both milky white and clear varieties), feldspar (pink to gray), and mica specimens at Purcell-Griffin Mine. Quartz is the most common and typically forms crystals on old mine dumps and exposed pegmatitic outcrops. Feldspar specimens are found in the granitic host rock, sometimes showing excellent color and luster when freshly broken.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Purcell-Griffin Mine in California?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Purcell-Griffin Mine. It is on BLM public land where recreational collecting is allowed under FLPMA regulations. You can collect up to 25 lbs per day at no cost, but commercial collecting and motorized excavation are prohibited.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit Purcell-Griffin Mine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for collecting at Purcell-Griffin Mine. Summer months bring extreme heat to the Sierra Nevada foothills, making daytime conditions uncomfortable. Fall and spring offer moderate temperatures perfect for hiking the rocky terrain and searching mineral exposures.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Purcell-Griffin Mine for beginners?
faq.answer: Purcell-Griffin Mine is beginner-friendly in terms of mineral identification and collection rules, but the terrain is moderately challenging. The rocky slopes and loose material require sturdy boots and careful footing. Beginners should start on the more accessible old mine dumps before attempting steeper hillside areas. The site is suitable for families with children ages 8 and up.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding near Mariposa at this location?
faq.answer: Bring a geological hammer and chisel for carefully extracting specimens from quartz veins. Sturdy hiking boots are essential due to rocky, uneven terrain. Pack at least 2-3 liters of water per person as the exposed foothill terrain offers minimal shade. A small collection bag or backpack, protective gloves, and a hand lens for specimen examination are also recommended.

faq.question: Is Purcell-Griffin Mine rockhounding site family and dog friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Purcell-Griffin Mine is family-friendly for rockhounders with children age 8 and older. The BLM land is also dog-friendly, so you can bring your pets along. However, be aware that the rocky terrain, loose material, abandoned mine structures, and heat exposure require supervision of children and pets. Keep dogs close to avoid hazards from old mine shafts and loose rock.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Purcell-Griffin Mine on BLM land near Mariposa. Free personal use collecting, no permit required. Explore California's mining heritage.
